steps.title: Identify the grammatical structure
steps.content: The sentence has subject Nepal’s constitution and main verb contains, but the prepositional phrase according to legal researchers James Melton and Tom Ginsburg interrupts between them. “According to” is the preposition; its object is the noun phrase legal researchers James Melton and Tom Ginsburg. The proper names serve as a restrictive appositive identifying which researchers, so no comma precedes them. Because the entire prepositional phrase is an interrupter, it must close with a comma before contains.

steps.title: Apply the appositive punctuation rule
steps.content:
When an appositive is essential to identify its noun—as these names are here, specifying which specific researchers are being cited—it is restrictive and requires no comma before it.

An intervening prepositional phrase must also close with a comma before the main verb contains.
Choice B (‘researchers James Melton and Tom Ginsburg,’) correctly applies this rule: “researchers James Melton and Tom Ginsburg,” with no internal comma but a closing comma.

The other choices violate the rule by either omitting the closing comma, incorrectly adding a comma before the restrictive appositive, or both.

That confirms B is correct.
